feedback.text: Current laws lag a lot behind things that are happening in our cyber side of lives. While local and European regulations are playing the catch-up games with social media sites, we are in the era of AI destroying the space that we built so meticulously. People were degraded to mere consumers, at least in the official language, but the reality is, we are just a product that is sold to tech overlords. The Internet became so popular, because it gave opportunities for everyone. But as more and more companies tried to monetize it, we ended up in a situation where everything is as centralized as it can be, where we own nothing, and give out everything, getting nothing back. Everyone, and our political elites especially, should do their best to guarantee to reverse the course of action. If we buy something, we own it. Nobody should be able to take it back or make it remotely unusable. Things like games, applications, web pages and more should be treated as a creation of people who made them, not as an asset of wealthy people who claim ownership over it. This sentiment was displayed well through "Stop Killing Games" action, but this extends well beyond games. With this as an introduction, I want to list causes that are, at least in my opinion, important for people to be happy about their digital life. - Digital preservation and archiving. Be it through new initiatives, inviting existing ones or financing them. - Digital freedom and independence, especially from tech overlords and USA based companies. - Extending "Stop Killing Games" ideas beyond games, to preserve our digital legacy. - Support for open initiatives, open source and open hardware through all means: legally, financially, and others. - Increasing support for security, and right to stay anonymous. - Giving parents tools and knowledge to better take care of their children in digital space. - Resistance against content serving algorithms. - Empowering people and organizations who decide to claim back their data, and start to self-host for themselves and their communities. - Punishing negligence from tech giants, especially in fields of user privacy, moderation and adhering to laws. - Giving less, preferably no, slack in terms of adhering to law for large companies, and especially tech giants. - Helping people to resist against tech giants plainly stealing to train their AI models. - Regulating AI to prevent issues, like mental health decline or hallucinations and people believing them. - Development and help with development of locally sourced software and hardware.
